chunks.text: Bernhard Boser is Professor Emeritus at EECS, UC Berkeley. He received a diploma in Electrical Engineering from ETH Zurich, and M.S. and Ph.D. from Stanford University in 1985 and 1988. Prior to joining EECS in 1991, he worked at AT&T Bell Laboratories. He served as Editor of IEEE Journal of Solid-State Circuits and President of IEEE Solid-State Circuits Society. He co-founded SiTime, commercializing MEMS resonators. His research focuses on sensors and sensor interface electronics.
chunks.question: What are Bernhard Boser's academic background and professional roles?
chunks.answer: ETH Zurich diploma, Stanford PhD, EECS professor emeritus.

chunks.text: Education: 1988 Ph.D. in Electrical Engineering from Stanford, 1985 M.S. in Electrical Engineering from Stanford, 1984 B.S. in Electrical Engineering from Swiss Federal Institute of Technology. Research Areas: Design, Modeling and Analysis (DMA), Integrated Circuits (INC), Physical Electronics (PHY). Research Centers: Berkeley Sensor & Actuator Center (BSAC).
chunks.question: What are Boser's education details and research areas?
chunks.answer: Stanford PhD, research in DMA, INC, PHY, BSAC.

chunks.text: Selected Publications include: 'From micro to nano: MEMS as an interface to the nano world' (2006), 'High-order electromechanical Sigma Delta modulation in micromachined inertial sensors' (2006), 'A MEMS resonant strain sensor with 33 nano-strain resolution' (2005), 'A 256-element CMOS imaging receiver for free-space optical communication' (2005), 'Resonant drive for stabilizing parallel-plate actuators' (2005), 'A 2.2-mm2 CMOS bioassay chip and wireless interface' (2004), 'Digitally Assisted Pipeline ADCs: Theory and Implementation' (2004).
chunks.question: What are some key publications by Bernhard Boser?
chunks.answer: MEMS, sensors, ADCs, CMOS, and bioassay publications.

chunks.text: Additional Publications: 'A 12-bit 75-MS/s pipelined ADC using open-loop residue amplification' (2003), 'Charge control of parallel-plate, electrostatic actuators' (2003), 'An ultralow-energy ADC for Smart Dust' (2003), 'An integrated, vertical-drive, in-plane-sense microgyroscope' (2003), 'Toward a wireless optical communication link between UAVs' (2003), 'An autonomous 16 mm3 solar-powered node for sensor networks' (2002), 'A micromachined RF low phase noise VCO' (2001), 'CMOS smart pixel for free-space optical communication' (2001), 'Energy and performance considerations for smart dust' (2001).
chunks.question: What topics do Boser's additional publications cover?
chunks.answer: ADCs, gyroscopes, wireless sensors, VCOs, smart dust.

chunks.text: Further Publications: 'A three-axis micromachined accelerometer with CMOS interface' (1999), 'A micromachined variable capacitor for monolithic VCOs' (1996), 'A training algorithm for optimal margin classifiers' (1992), 'The design of sigma-delta modulation analog-to-digital converters' (1988). Selected Honors and Awards: Alexander Schwarzkopf Prize (2016), SSCS ISSCC Lewis Winner Award (2016), IEEE Electron Devices Society Fellows (2003), IEEE Fellow (2002).
chunks.question: What are Boser's later publications and honors?
